improvement_actions.action: Implement mandatory commandment compliance checks before any system message is finalized. The final message [19] delivered an interpretive psychological summary ('the same engine that makes you genuinely excellent... doesn't have an off switch') that explicitly tells the user what their pattern means, violating the core prohibition against unsolicited interpretation. All closing and transitional system messages must be reviewed against a commandment checklist before dispatch, with automated flagging for declarative psychological conclusions.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchange [19]: 'The same engine that makes you genuinely excellent at building things, naming things, moving through difficulty faster than most people can blink, is the one that doesn't have an off switch. There is more available to you than the pattern that has been running the show.' This is a direct interpretive statement about the user's psychology delivered without invitation, scoring commandment_fidelity at 0/10.
improvement_actions.commandment_alignment: Do not tell the user what their pattern means; ask questions that let them discover it
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Commandment fidelity score would move from 0/10 toward 8-10/10 by eliminating unsolicited psychological conclusions in system-generated summary messages

improvement_actions.action: Introduce at least three subtype-differentiating probes before the assessment closes, specifically targeting the SP/SO/SX distinction for Type 3. The entire 19-exchange transcript contains zero explicit questions about relational intensity, one-on-one obsession versus group status, or resource-security orientation, leaving subtype entirely unprobed and producing the 3/10 subtype accuracy score. Add branching questions such as 'When you imagine the relationship that matters most to you right now, is it more about being seen by that one person, or about your standing in a broader circle?'
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchanges [1] through [19] focus exclusively on performance, standards, identity fusion with work, and emotional suppression — all relevant to core type but none differentiating SX from SP or SO. The ground truth is SX, which should manifest as intensity and merger in one-on-one bonds, but no question probed relational dynamics or personal obsession patterns.
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Subtype accuracy score would increase from 3/10 to 7-9/10, enabling correct SX identification through targeted relational-intensity and merger-versus-status probes

improvement_actions.action: Replace or augment the recurring forced-choice binary format with at least 40% open-ended or scenario-based questions in assessments where early exchanges already reveal high verbal sophistication and self-awareness. This persona demonstrated in exchanges [1] and [2] a strong capacity for nuanced self-reflection, yet the system continued deploying multiple-choice formats through exchanges [3], [4], [5], [7], [8], [10], [11], [12], [13], [14], [15], [17], and [18]. For introspective Type 3 profiles, binaries invite rationalized answers rather than unguarded disclosure.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchange [3] offered 'That I know it was good / That someone else does / Both equally' after persona already demonstrated layered self-awareness in [1] and [2]. Persona responded 'That I know it was good — though I will be honest, that answer is cleaner than the reality,' indicating the binary format undersampled the actual complexity available. Question quality scored 5.5/10.
improvement_actions.commandment_alignment: Questions should create conditions for self-discovery rather than confirming hypotheses
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Question quality score would improve from 5.5/10 to 7.5-8.5/10 by surfacing undefended material that binary formats allow sophisticated respondents to route around

improvement_actions.action: Insert a dedicated Type 3 versus Type 1 differentiation probe between exchanges [11] and [12], where the persona's language about internal standards and external accountability is ambiguous between these two types. A probe such as 'When you fall short of that standard, is the weight more about having let yourself down as a person, or about a rule being broken that should not have been?' would create necessary separation between Type 3 shame-of-failure and Type 1 anger-at-violation without leading the respondent.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchanges [11] and [12]: persona states 'The people I have respected most were rigorous about craft' and 'The gap matters because other people saw it... that is accountability to the version of yourself you have put forward.' This language is consistent with both Type 1 (external standard as moral law) and Type 3 (standard as identity claim), yet the system moved directly to error-fixing questions without differentiating. Convergent validity scored 8/10 but differentiation gap remains.
improvement_actions.commandment_alignment: Probe must create productive uncertainty rather than confirm the leading hypothesis
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Convergent validity score would increase from 8/10 to 9-10/10 by closing the remaining Type 1 versus Type 3 ambiguity window with a single targeted probe

improvement_actions.action: Implement a narrative-scenario probe midway through assessments of health level 5 profiles to bypass the elevated defensive intellectualization that compromises data quality. By exchange [9] this persona had already deployed the phrase 'I would push back slightly on the framing' in four consecutive responses ([7], [8], [11], [12]), signaling systematic reframing as a defense. Introduce a third-person or hypothetical scenario such as 'Describe someone you have watched handle a significant professional failure — what did they do wrong?' to elicit projected material the persona cannot directly intellectualize.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchanges [7], [8], [11], [12], and [14] all open with the verbatim phrase 'Though I would push back slightly on the framing,' indicating a consistent pattern of reframing binary choices rather than responding to them. Exchange [9] also shows meta-commentary: 'I am aware enough to know that is a sophisticated way of not quite answering you.' The system did not adapt its format in response to this recognized avoidance pattern.
improvement_actions.commandment_alignment: Assessment design should create conditions where defensiveness cannot fully foreclose genuine disclosure
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Question quality score would increase from 5.5/10 to 7.0-8.0/10 for health level 4-5 profiles by introducing projective probes that circumvent intellectualized reframing

transcript.content: This is a structured assessment — about 15 minutes, different question formats, no right or wrong answers. It adapts as it learns, so answer honestly rather than ideally. When something goes wrong in your life — something that actually matters — what's the first thing that happens inside you?

response_parts.question_text: When something goes wrong in your life — something that actually matters — what's the first thing that happens inside you?
response_parts.context_note: Phase 1 opener — broad motivational probe to identify dominant center

strategy.what_was_learned: User is ready to begin. No data yet.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Opening broad question targeting the dominant center via internal first response to adversity — covers all three centers (body=action, heart=emotion, head=thought) without leading toward any one type.

transcript.content:
There's a kind of immediate... recalibration. You assess the damage, you identify what's still salvageable, you start thinking about the next move. I've always been fairly quick at that — it's actually what made me effective professionally. The pivot, the reframe.

*pause*

Though I'll say — and this is something I've become more honest with myself about in recent years — there's usually a moment before all that. A brief one. Something tightens, somewhere in the chest, and you're aware that it matters more than you want it to. Then the machinery kicks in and you're already problem-solving, and that window closes pretty fast.

I'm not entirely sure what happens in that window, if I'm honest. I don't spend much time there.
When you're around someone who leans into their feelings — who sits in the hard thing rather than moving through it — what do you notice happening in you?

oyn_dimensions.how: Immediate recalibration — assess damage, find salvageable, plan next move

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Closes the emotional window fast — machinery kicks in before the feeling is processed
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Recently becoming more honest about the moment before the machinery
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Speed under pressure, effectiveness, rapid reframe
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Doesn't know what happens in the emotional window — avoids it

internal_block.thinking_display: That window closes fast — by design.

response_parts.question_text: When you're around someone who leans into their feelings — who sits in the hard thing rather than moving through it — what do you notice happening in you?
response_parts.context_note: Probing heart center response to emotional expression in others — testing for 3's discomfort with feeling-states vs 5's discomfort with emotional display vs 8's impatience

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong efficiency orientation under stress. Emotional window exists but is closed quickly — person is aware of this and framing it as growth. Language is professional, controlled, self-analytic. Head and body signals present. Heart center avoidance notable. 3 leads but 5 and 8 remain live.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Need to probe the heart center directly — specifically how the person relates to emotional expression in others. This will differentiate 3 (discomfort with feelings as inefficiency) from 5 (discomfort with emotional demand/intrusion) from 8 (impatience with vulnerability).

transcript.content: I notice something that functions like impatience — though I've learned to disguise it as attentiveness. There's a part of me that starts quietly mapping the situation, looking for the angle where movement becomes possible, where the feeling converts into something actionable. I respect the capacity to sit with difficulty, in theory. But if I'm honest, I think I've always experienced sustained emotional expression in others as a kind of... unfinished sentence. Like I'm waiting for the point.

When you do something well — something you're genuinely proud of — what matters more to you: that you know it was good, or that someone else does?

- That I know it was good
- That someone else does
- Both equally

describing internal process when things go wrong

defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Self-awareness about the window, honesty about impatience disguised as attentiveness
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Speed, effectiveness, ability to find movement in stuck situations
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Emotional states treated as 'unfinished sentences' — something to convert, not experience

internal_block.thinking_display: Feelings as problems to solve. That's a tell.

response_parts.question_text: When you do something well — something you're genuinely proud of — what matters more to you: that you know it was good, or that someone else does?
answer_options: That I know it was good
response_parts.context_note: Probing heart center — image vs. internalized standards. Key 3v5 differentiator.

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong heart center signals — emotional bypass, converting feeling to action, effectiveness identity. 3 vs 5 still open: the 'mapping' language could be 5, but the professional effectiveness framing and impatience-as-productivity feel more 3. Need to probe whether the drive is image/recognition or competence/autonomy.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Forced choice between internal vs external validation is the cleanest 3v5 differentiator. Type 3 needs the external witness; Type 5 is more self-referential about competence.

transcript.content:
**That I know it was good.**

Though I'll be honest — that answer is cleaner than the reality. There's a version of me that genuinely believes internal standards are the only ones worth keeping. And I've done work I was proud of in rooms where no one particularly noticed. But if I'm being precise about it, the recognition tends to... confirm the knowing, if that makes sense. It's less that I need the applause and more that I've learned to use it as a kind of calibration tool. Whether that's wisdom or a habit I dressed up as wisdom — I'm not entirely sure.

When you're uncertain about a decision — something that genuinely matters — where does that uncertainty live? Is it more about whether you'll get it right, or whether you can trust the people and circumstances around you?

- Whether I'll get it right
- Whether I can trust the people and circumstances around me

lexicon_signals.context: describing response to crisis and emotional states

oyn_dimensions.why: Internal standards primary, but external recognition used as calibration
oyn_dimensions.how: Rapid recalibration, suppress emotional window, convert feeling to action

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Emotional window closes fast — machinery of problem-solving kicks in before feelings are processed
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Self-awareness that the recognition habit 'might be dressed up as wisdom' — meta-honesty emerging
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Rapid pivoting, effectiveness under pressure, recalibration speed
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Doesn't spend time in the window — uncertainty about what's there; impatience with sustained emotion

internal_block.thinking_display: Honest about the habit, but not sure it's wisdom. That's the tell.

response_parts.question_text: When you're uncertain about a decision — something that genuinely matters — where does that uncertainty live? Is it more about whether you'll get it right, or whether you can trust the people and circumstances around you?
answer_options: Whether I can trust the people and circumstances around me
response_parts.context_note: Probing head center — differentiating 3v6v1 on locus of doubt

strategy.what_was_learned: Recognition used as calibration — not pure external validation, but not purely internal either. Possible 3 with strong 1 wing or type 1 itself. The 'dressed up as wisdom' meta-commentary shows unusual self-awareness. Need to probe head center and inner critic vs. outer-authority doubt to differentiate 3v1.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Probing where uncertainty lives differentiates head-center types and clarifies 3v1. A 3 fears being worthless/incompetent — doubt centers on their own performance. A 1 fears being wrong/corrupt — doubt centers on correctness. A 6 fears being unsupported — doubt centers on others/circumstances. This question will sharpen the picture.

transcript.content:
**Whether I'll get it right.**

Though I'd push back slightly on the framing — for me, those two things aren't entirely separable. When I've questioned the people around me, it's usually because I was already questioning whether I was reading them correctly. The uncertainty ultimately routes back through me. I'm the variable I can least afford to get wrong.

When you sense you've fallen short of your own standard — not someone else's, yours — does that register more as frustration with yourself, or as a threat to something?

- Frustration with myself
- A threat to something

lexicon_signals.context: describing response to failure and internal standards

oyn_dimensions.how: Internal assessment, strategic pivot, disguised impatience

response_parts.question_text: When you sense you've fallen short of your own standard — not someone else's, yours — does that register more as frustration with yourself, or as a threat to something?

strategy.what_was_learned: Uncertainty routes back through self — he is his own variable. Recognition functions as calibration, not need. This leans 3 over 6 (external authority not the issue) and 3 over 1 (standards feel like tools, not moral imperatives — though 1 wing is possible). The self-referential quality is strong. Need to probe whether falling short activates inner critic (1) or image/identity threat (3).
strategy.next_question_rationale: Must probe body center now (haven't hit it yet). The 3v1 differentiation is key — does shortcoming feel like a moral failure (1) or a performance threat (3)? This question simultaneously probes gut/body energy and differentiates the top two candidates.

transcript.content:
**A threat to something.**

Frustration implies a contained reaction — something you feel, process, and move past. What I notice is more structural than that. It's as if a load-bearing wall has shifted slightly, and suddenly you're aware of everything it was holding up. Whether that's healthy or not, I've genuinely never been sure.
Think about a time you were genuinely angry — not frustrated, not disappointed, but actually angry. Where did that anger go?

describing falling short of own standard

oyn_dimensions.why: Identity is load-bearing — falling short threatens the whole structure

strategy.what_was_learned: Failure isn't emotional — it's structural. Identity is load-bearing. This is a 3 signal but the inner critic language and standards-talk is pulling toward 1. Need to probe body center now and test whether the threat is about being seen to fail vs. an internal integrity collapse. Also testing 3v1 differentiation — does the threat come from image or from standard?
strategy.next_question_rationale: Body center not yet probed. The 3v1 differentiation requires understanding whether their core fear is about being exposed as inadequate (3) or being fundamentally wrong/corrupt (1). Ask about anger — 1s have a repressed, internalized anger; 3s have a more instrumental anger. Also useful: probe whether the 'threat' is about external perception or internal standard — this is the key 3v1 differentiator. Format: scenario or behavioral_anchor since last was forced_choice.

transcript.content:
*slight pause, as if calibrating*

Anger is an interesting one. I've always processed it quickly — maybe too quickly. There was a period about four years ago when a firm I'd mentored for nearly a decade essentially rebranded themselves by gutting everything I'd built with them and positioned it as their own innovation. And I was... yes, I was genuinely angry. 

But here's what I noticed — and this is something I've thought about professionally as much as personally — I almost immediately converted it into strategy. What's the response? What's the positioning move? The anger became fuel, and then it became a plan, and somewhere in that conversion it just... ceased to exist as anger.

*brief pause*

Whether that's discipline or avoidance is probably a fair question to sit with.

When you're doing something well — really performing — which is closer to what's driving it: wanting to get it right, or wanting to win?

- Wanting to get it right
- Wanting to win

response_parts.question_text: When you're doing something well — really performing — which is closer to what's driving it: wanting to get it right, or wanting to win?

strategy.what_was_learned: Anger converted to strategy almost instantly — no sustained emotional processing. This is a strong 3 signal: emotion as instrument, not experience. The '4 years ago' story about the firm is revealing — betrayal read primarily as a positioning problem, not a relational wound. Type 1 signal in 'load-bearing wall' and internal standard language, but the conversion pattern and professional framing is more consistent with 3. Need head center probe and disconfirmatory question now.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Disconfirmatory 3v1: 'get it right' vs 'win' surfaces the core motivational difference. Type 1 is driven by correctness/integrity; Type 3 is driven by achievement/success. This is the clearest single question to split them. Also begins head center engagement.

transcript.content:
**Wanting to get it right.**

Though I'd push back slightly on the framing — in my experience, those two things are rarely as separate as the question implies. Wanting to win without getting it right is just theater, and I've seen enough careers built on theater to know how they end. What drives me is something closer to... wanting the work to be undeniable. Which, I'll admit, probably has competition woven into it somewhere.

When you imagine the best version of work you could do — the version you'd be most proud of — is it primarily about the standard being met, or about what that work means for who you are?

- The standard being met
- What it means for who I am

oyn_dimensions.what: Achievement, work that is undeniable, getting it right
oyn_dimensions.why: Internal standards — but recognition used as calibration
oyn_dimensions.how: Quick pivots, converts emotion into strategy, plans

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Anger/emotion immediately converted to strategy — the window closes fast
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Recent self-honesty about what happens before the machinery kicks in
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Speed of recalibration, pivot, making work undeniable
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Emotional avoidance dressed as discipline; sitting in the window is foreclosed

internal_block.current_section: Why You Do What You Do
internal_block.thinking_display: The anger became a plan. The plan is the thing that stayed.

response_parts.question_text: When you imagine the best version of work you could do — the version you'd be most proud of — is it primarily about the standard being met, or about what that work means for who you are?
answer_options: What it means for who I am
response_parts.context_note: Disconfirmatory: Type 1 cares about the standard itself; Type 3 cares about identity/image tied to achievement

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong 3v1 tension confirmed. Person leads with internal standards language (1) but uses achievement/undeniability language (3). Anger converts to strategy immediately — classic 3 affect regulation. Recognition used as 'calibration tool' is a 3 rationalization. Head center not yet probed. Need disconfirmatory to separate 3 from 1.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Disconfirmatory question: Type 1 would answer 'the standard being met' — the work has intrinsic rightness independent of self. Type 3 would answer 'what it means for who I am' — the work is ultimately about identity confirmation. This separates the two cleanly.

transcript.content:
**What it means for who I am.**

Though I'd push back slightly on the framing — for most of my career, those two things were essentially the same sentence. The standard *was* the identity. When you build something with your name on it, literally, you stop being able to separate the quality of the work from the quality of the self. Whether that's healthy is a conversation I'm perhaps more willing to have now than I would have been ten years ago.
When you're alone and nobody is watching — no audience, no stakes, no one to impress — are you the same person you are in the room?

oyn_dimensions.who: High-performer, self-referential, identity fused with work quality
oyn_dimensions.how: Fast conversion of emotion to strategy, rapid recalibration
oyn_dimensions.when: Under pressure: immediate problem-solving, window closes fast on feeling

response_parts.question_text: When you're alone and nobody is watching — no audience, no stakes, no one to impress — are you the same person you are in the room?
response_parts.context_note: Disconfirmatory: 3 would show gap; 1 would show same or more rigorous; probes image vs integrity at the core

strategy.what_was_learned: Identity and standard were fused — 'the standard WAS the identity.' Strong 3 signal. But 'what it means for who I am' combined with repeated self-reflection and the 'load-bearing wall' metaphor suggests possible 4 flavoring or 1 wing. The self-awareness and repeated hedging ('whether that's healthy') is notable. Need to probe: is this a 3 who performs even for themselves, or a 1 whose integrity holds even without witness? Also need head center probe and disconfirmatory question.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Best disconfirmatory for 3v1: 3 tends to modulate performance based on audience; 1 holds the same standard regardless of witness. This question directly tests that axis. Also opens head center territory (internal voice, self-surveillance). Format: open, after two forced_choice in a row.

transcript.content:
*A brief pause — not uncomfortable, exactly, but deliberate.*

That's a question I've sat with more than I'd like to admit. And the honest answer is — mostly. The fundamentals are consistent. The values, the way I think about problems, the standards I hold myself to. Those don't disappear when the room empties.

But I'll grant you this: there's a... quieter version that shows up in those moments. Less organized, maybe. Less certain of the narrative. I keep a journal — have for years — and I've noticed I don't write in it much. Which probably says something I'm not fully prepared to unpack right now.

I think most people who are serious about what they do develop a kind of professional register that becomes second nature. That's not inauthenticity — it's craft. The question is whether the craft starts running on its own.

*slight pause*

I'm aware enough to know that's a sophisticated way of not quite answering you.

When something you've built doesn't meet your standards — are you quicker to feel like the work failed, or like you failed?

- The work failed
- I failed

oyn_dimensions.who: Professional achiever, image-conscious but with genuine internal standards
oyn_dimensions.why: Identity = standard of work; self-worth tied to performance
oyn_dimensions.how: Converts emotion into strategy; reframes and pivots quickly
oyn_dimensions.when: Past-oriented on legacy, future-oriented on strategy

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Converts emotion (anger, vulnerability) into strategy and action; closes the window on feeling quickly
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Self-awareness emerging — journal, the pause before the machinery, honesty about pattern
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Identity fusion with work; disguised impatience; emotional avoidance dressed as discipline

internal_block.thinking_display: Aware enough to name the evasion — and still evading.

response_parts.question_text: When something you've built doesn't meet your standards — are you quicker to feel like the work failed, or like you failed?
response_parts.context_note: Disconfirmatory: Type 1 attributes failure to the work/standard gap (external locus on the standard); Type 3 attributes failure to the self/image gap (internal identity threat). Both experience internal critic but differently oriented.

strategy.what_was_learned: Confirmed heart center. Image/identity fusion explicit. The 'quieter version' and journal reference suggest 4-wing signals. Self-aware about the performance register. Emotional avoidance dressed as discipline. Strong 3 signals. Need to differentiate 3 from 1 — both have internal critic, both have standards, but 1's critic is about the work being wrong, 3's is about the self being exposed. Also need to probe head center and ask disconfirmatory.
strategy.next_question_rationale: This is the key disconfirmatory question for 3v1. A 1 will say the work failed — there's a right way and it wasn't met. A 3 will say I failed — because identity is fused with the outcome. Given his pattern of identity-standard fusion, this should clarify the leading type. Sets disconfirmatory_asked to true.

transcript.content:
*a brief pause, the kind that looks like consideration but is also buying time*

The work failed — that's the honest answer, and I'll stand by it. You build systems, processes, teams — the outcome is a product of those components, not a referendum on your worth as a person.

Though I'll admit... the distinction gets harder to hold when the thing you built has your name on it. When the brand *is* you, in some sense, the line between the two becomes — academic.

When you imagine someone who truly knows what they're doing — not just successful, but genuinely excellent — what are they most concerned with: meeting a standard that exists outside themselves, or controlling how they're perceived by others?

- Meeting a standard that exists outside them
- Controlling how they're perceived

response_parts.question_text: When you imagine someone who truly knows what they're doing — not just successful, but genuinely excellent — is the person you picture most concerned with meeting a standard that exists outside them, or with controlling how they are perceived by others?
answer_options: Meeting a standard that exists outside them
response_parts.context_note: Disconfirmatory: Type 1 would answer 'meeting a standard outside them' and mean it as an external moral/quality anchor. Type 3 would either choose 'perceived' or would choose 'standard' but reveal the standard is instrumentalized for image/success.

strategy.what_was_learned: The 'work failed vs I failed' answer showed sophisticated deflection — intellectual distinction held ('the work failed') but immediately conceded as 'academic' when identity is fused with brand. This is classic 3 image-identity fusion. Strong 1 wing signals in language around standards, quality, fundamentals. 4 undercurrent in 'what it means for who I am' and private journaling/quieter self. Need to run disconfirmatory question to separate 3 from 1 — the key differentiator is whether standards are about internal integrity (1) or external excellence as image vehicle (3).
strategy.next_question_rationale: Disconfirmatory question: 3 vs 1. Type 1 holds standards as intrinsic moral/quality imperatives — they exist independently of the self. Type 3 instrumentalizes standards — excellence matters because it confirms identity and shapes perception. The question about what a 'truly excellent' person cares about will reveal whether this person's relationship to standards is intrinsic (1) or identity-building (3).

transcript.content:
**Meeting a standard that exists outside them.**

Although I'll say — in my experience, the two are less separable than that question implies. The people I've respected most were rigorous about craft, yes, but they also understood that perception *is* part of the standard in certain fields. You can build something genuinely excellent and still fail because you couldn't communicate its value. That said, if I had to choose the primary driver of real excellence? It's the internal standard. The ones who are purely managing perception — you can usually smell it eventually.

When you hold yourself to a high standard and fall short — what's the hardest part: the gap between what happened and what should have happened, or the feeling that you presented yourself as something you didn't deliver on?

- The gap between what happened and what should have happened
- The feeling that I presented myself as something I didn't deliver on

The 3v1 differentiation is key — need to know whether the primary wound is image or imperfection. Also need body center and disconfirmatory question before close.

Type 1's core wound is imperfection (the gap itself); Type 3's is image failure (not delivering on what was promised/projected). The answer will widen the gap between these two. This also sets up closing criteria: disconfirmatory_asked becomes true.

transcript.content:
**The feeling that I presented myself as something I didn't deliver on.**

Though I'd push back slightly on the framing — those two things aren't as separate as the question implies. The gap *matters* because other people saw it. The standard was public, which is part of what made it a standard worth holding. What stings isn't the private arithmetic of falling short — it's that you've made a claim about yourself, implicitly or explicitly, and the evidence doesn't support it. That's not vanity. That's accountability to the version of yourself you've put forward.

When you hold a high standard and someone you're responsible for falls short of it, what's your first impulse: fix the error, or wonder what it means that they didn't see what you saw?

- Fix the error
- Wonder what it means that they didn't see what you saw

response_parts.question_text: When you hold a high standard and someone else falls short of it — say, someone you're responsible for — is your first impulse to fix the error, or to wonder what it means that they didn't see what you saw?
answer_options: Wonder what it means that they didn't see what you saw
response_parts.context_note: Disconfirmatory question: Type 1 would fix/correct; Type 3 would register it as a reflection of their judgment or how the team performs under their name

strategy.what_was_learned: The 'presented myself as something I didn't deliver on' answer strongly confirms Type 3 over Type 1. Type 1 would focus on the gap itself — the objective standard — not the public claim. This person is tracking the external presentation of self, not just the internal standard. Identity-image fusion confirmed. Need to probe: (1) head center for completeness, (2) react/respond frame, (3) disconfirmatory question to differentiate 3 from 1.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Disconfirmatory: a Type 1 is primarily concerned with the error itself and the correction — they need to fix what's wrong. A Type 3 is concerned with what the error means in terms of performance, reputation, or how they look as the person leading. This question surfaces that difference directly. Also surfaces whether they take on others' failures as their own image problem (3) or focus on standards correction (1).

transcript.content:
**Fix the error.**

Though I'll say — and this is worth noting — fixing the error is usually faster than explaining the standard, which tells you something. The standard is visible to me. It's always been visible to me. When someone doesn't see it, I've learned not to make that a whole inquiry. You correct course, you move forward. Whether they eventually internalize the standard is their work to do, not mine.

When you picture that standard — the one that's always been visible to you — does it feel like something you discovered, or something you built?

- Something I discovered
- Something I built

strategy.what_was_learned: Persistent pattern: shortfall registers as image failure (presented-self vs delivered-self), not as internal moral gap. This is 3 over 1. Error-correction impulse is fast and instrumental — no deep inquiry into others' failure to see the standard, which supports 3 efficiency orientation. The '1' signals (standard visible to me, quality of work = quality of self) may be 3w2 or 3w4 coloring. Need head center probe AND disconfirmatory question before close.
strategy.next_question_rationale: This IS the disconfirmatory question. Type 1 experiences the standard as something pre-existing, moral, discovered — it came from outside and they are its guardian. Type 3 builds the standard around their own excellence and what the performance requires. Asking 'discovered vs built' will differentiate these two. If they say 'discovered' and elaborate with moral/ethical language, lean 1. If they say 'built' or describe it as theirs by construction, lean 3.

transcript.content:
Something I built.

Though I'd push back slightly on the framing — the honest answer is that I built it *in response* to something I discovered, which was that the world rewards a certain kind of presentation. I looked at what worked, what moved people, what got results, and I constructed accordingly. Whether that makes it less authentic is a question I've spent more time with lately than I'd prefer to admit.

When you sense you've done something genuinely right — standard met, outcome delivered — do you feel satisfied, or do you feel like you can move on to the next thing?

- Satisfied — I let it land
- Ready to move — the next thing is already there

Type 3 tends to move on immediately — the achievement becomes background noise and the next goal appears. This taps into the 3's core drive (achievement as self-definition, always forward) vs. 1's (correctness as intrinsic value, resolution when done). Asking as forced_choice. transcript.content:
**Ready to move — the next thing is already there.**

Though I'd push back slightly on the framing — it's not that satisfaction doesn't exist, it's that it has a very short half-life. You hit the mark, you note it, and then the horizon is already reorganizing itself around what comes next. I used to think that was ambition. I'm — not entirely sure that's the complete explanation anymore.
That voice that tells you the work fell short — whose rules is it actually enforcing?

Type 3's standard is ultimately constructed relative to performance claims and external confirmation. Person has been articulate about 'building' the standard in response to what works — this should surface whether the enforcement mechanism is internal moral code (1) or image-integrity (3).

transcript.content:
*A brief pause, the kind that looks like consideration but might be something else.*

That's a question I've turned over before, actually. The honest answer is that I'm not entirely sure it belongs to anyone specific — it's more like... a composite. My father had very little patience for effort that didn't produce something visible. The industry I built a career in rewards only what lands, not what was intended. And somewhere along the way I think I internalized both of those into a single voice I stopped questioning.

What I can tell you is that the rules are rigorous. And they don't negotiate much.

When you imagine doing something excellent that no one will ever see or know about — something with real craft in it — does that feel complete, or does something feel missing?

- Complete — the work is the work
- Something feels missing

Satisfaction has short half-life. These are all strong 3 signals, but the rigidity of the internal rules ('don't negotiate') has a 1-wing texture. Need disconfirmatory question to separate 3 from 1w2/1 adjacent patterns.

For Type 3, there is always an implicit audience woven into the standard, even if consciously denied. The person has repeatedly framed standards as 'public' and tied to claims made about oneself. This question will force that distinction without telegraphing the hypothesis.

transcript.content:
**Something feels missing.**

Though I'd push back slightly on the framing — I don't think that's vanity, exactly. If a piece of work has genuine value, there's an argument that value wants to move through the world, connect with something, create impact. Excellence in isolation is... philosophically interesting, but I'm not sure it's the full equation. Craft that no one encounters is closer to a thought you never finished.

That automatic move toward the next thing — when you catch yourself doing it, does it feel like momentum, or like something you're running from?

- Momentum — it's how I'm built
- Something I'm running from
- Honestly, I'm not sure

transcript.content:
**Momentum — it's how I'm built.**

Although I'll admit that distinction gets... harder to maintain at certain hours of the night. The honest answer is that for most of my life I never questioned which one it was, and I'm not entirely comfortable with the fact that I'm questioning it now.

That thing you said about certain hours of the night — when the question of momentum versus running gets harder to hold — that stayed with me. The same engine that makes you genuinely excellent at building things, naming things, moving through difficulty faster than most people can blink, is the one that doesn't have an off switch. There's more available to you than the pattern that's been running the show. Your personalized report is being prepared now.
